unitTitle: KS2 Y5 Selection in physical computing (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Connecting Crumbles
lessons.objectives:
I can build a simple circuit and connect it to a microcontroller
I can program a microcontroller to make an LED switch on
I can explain what an infinite loop does
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: microcontroller · Crumble · Sparkle (an LED) · component · connect · infinite loop. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a Crumble, a Sparkle, a battery box and crocodile leads per pair; the Crumble software open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — meet the Crumble. Show the Crumble board. Pupils LABEL the board (drag USB port / power pads / A B C D pads / motor outputs onto the picture) and choose what a microcontroller is.
   • Support: choose from a word bank.  • Core: recall what a Sparkle is.  • Challenge: multi-select what a microcontroller can do.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — build the circuit. Connect the battery box and the Sparkle to the Crumble with crocodile leads (+ to +, – to –, D to D). Likely error: + lead on a – pad → fix-words “match plus to plus, minus to minus.” The Sparkle flashes white 6 times when it is right.
3. (20–35) You-do — program the Sparkle. Build a program to turn the Sparkle on. Then put it inside a “do forever” loop so it flashes for ever. Pupils ORDER the build steps and build the Parsons program on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: order 3 steps / choose the loop block.  • Core: explain what “do forever” does.  • Challenge: change colours and timings.
4. (35–50) Make it flash — pupils download the program to the Crumble, test it, and screenshot their blocks onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; rate how confident you feel.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Combining output components
lessons.objectives:
I can connect more than one output component to a microcontroller
I can use a count-controlled loop to control outputs
I can design a sequence that uses a count-controlled loop
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: output component · motor · Sparkle · repetition · count-controlled loop · algorithm. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a Crumble, a Sparkle, a geared motor + wheel and a spinning disc per pair. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — recap. SORT parts into input / output / not connected to the Crumble yet, then choose what a motor is.
   • Support: choose output or not.  • Core: name two output components.  • Challenge: explain the difference between a Sparkle and a motor.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — connect a motor and a Sparkle. Show the wiring diagram. The motor goes on the + and – pads near number 1; the Sparkle stays on D. The motor command can be forwards, backwards or stop, with a power %. Likely error: motor spins the wrong way → fix-words “swap the red and black motor leads.”
3. (20–35) Count-controlled loop. A “do … times” loop repeats the blocks inside it. Pupils build the Parsons count-controlled program and ORDER an algorithm into steps on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: choose the repeat number / order 3 steps.  • Core: fill the blank in the loop count.  • Challenge: design a sequence of their own.
4. (35–50) You-do — “disco dancer”. Build the program, run it on the Crumble, and screenshot the blocks onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; rate confidence.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Controlling with conditions
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a condition is either true or false
I can design a conditional loop
I can program a microcontroller to respond to an input
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: condition · true · false · switch · input · do until loop. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a Crumble, a Sparkle and a push switch per pair. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — true or false? A condition is a sentence that can only be true or false. Pupils SORT sentences into “can be true/false” vs “not a condition”, and turn statements into yes/no questions.
   • Support: choose true or false.  • Core: write one condition of their own.  • Challenge: explain why “What is the weather?” is not a condition.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — the switch is an input. Show the wiring with a push switch. Pressing the switch completes the circuit; the Crumble reads it as “A is HI”. The switch is an INPUT.
3. (22–40) Conditional loop. A “do until” loop keeps going UNTIL its condition is true. Pupils build the Parsons “do until A is HI” program and MATCH loop words to meanings on the activity worksheet. Likely error: mixing up “while/until” → fix-words “do UNTIL the condition is true, then stop.”
   • Support: match the words.  • Core: fill the blank condition.  • Challenge: design a do-until loop for a real situation.
4. (40–50) You-do — run it. Press the switch to stop the loop; screenshot the blocks onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; rate confidence.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Starting with selection
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a condition being met can start an action
I can identify a condition and an action
I can use selection (an ‘if…then…’ statement) to direct a program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: selection · if…then · condition · action · input · output. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a Crumble, a Sparkle and a switch per pair. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — condition and action. In “if it is raining, then put up your umbrella”, the condition starts the action. Pupils SORT phrases into condition vs action, and choose which part is the condition.
   • Support: choose condition or action.  • Core: write one ‘if…then…’ sentence.  • Challenge: explain why a loop keeps checking the condition.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — what is selection? Selection means: IF a condition is true, THEN do an action. Show the if…then code (if A is HI then flash). Likely error: thinking selection stops the loop → fix-words “selection CHOOSES whether to do the action; the loop keeps checking.”
3. (22–40) You-do — write selection. Pupils build the Parsons ‘if…then…’ program (inside a forever loop) and FILL the blanks of an ‘if…then…’ sentence on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: choose the missing block.  • Core: fill the if and then blanks.  • Challenge: add a second action after “then”.
4. (40–50) Run it. Press the switch; the actions only happen when the condition is true. Screenshot the blocks. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; rate confidence.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Drawing designs
lessons.objectives:
I can identify a real-world example of a condition starting an action
I can describe what my project will do
I can create a detailed, labelled drawing of my project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: selection · condition · action · design · input · output · wiring diagram. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the demonstration carousel model to look at; design paper; the Crumble kits. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — selection in the real world. Lights that come on when it gets dark, doors that open when someone approaches — a condition starts an action. Pupils MATCH real-world conditions to their actions, and choose true examples of selection.
   • Support: match condition to action.  • Core: write one real-world ‘if…then…’.  • Challenge: multi-select which everyday devices use selection.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — the project. Show the fairground carousel photo and the demo model. The task: build a carousel that uses SELECTION to start when a switch is pressed, with at least two output devices.
3. (22–45) You-do — design it. On the activity worksheet pupils describe what their model will do, ORDER the design steps, and plan their input and outputs. They draw a labelled diagram and a wiring plan (on paper), then screenshot/photograph it.
   • Support: tick the parts to include.  • Core: fill in the input and outputs.  • Challenge: write the ‘if…then…’ their model will use. Likely error: forgetting the input → fix-words “selection needs a condition — what is the input?”
4. (45–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; rate confidence; share one design with a partner.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Writing and testing algorithms
lessons.objectives:
I can write an algorithm that describes what my model will do
I can use selection to produce an intended outcome
I can test and debug my project
lessons.outline:
Routine first (final lesson of the unit). Vocabulary on board: algorithm · selection · condition · action · test · debug. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; their model, design plan and circuit from last lesson; the Crumble software. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — clear and precise algorithms. Compare a vague algorithm with a precise one (timings, repeats, motor power). Pupils choose the more precise algorithm and ORDER an algorithm into steps.
   • Support: choose precise or vague.  • Core: add one missing detail.  • Challenge: explain why precise algorithms help the programmer.
2. (10–25) Write the algorithm. Pupils write the algorithm for their carousel, using selection (if switch pressed then spin + flash), repeatedly checked in a forever loop, with waits and outputs turned off at the end.
3. (25–45) You-do — write and test the program. Build the program from the algorithm; test a few blocks at a time; build the Parsons ‘if…then…’ program. Likely error: program never starts → fix-words “check the input — is the switch on the right pads, is the condition ‘A is HI’?” Screenshot the working blocks.
   • Support: choose the bug.  • Core: fill the blank in the fix.  • Challenge: write what you tested and what you changed.
4. (45–52) Test and debug + evaluate — does the model do what the task asked? What would you improve?
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; rate confidence.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
